description: The assigned key `event` comes from a fixed, hardcoded `events` array of known AJAX callback names (e.g. 'success', 'error'), not from user-controlled input, and `this.options` is a freshly cloned plain object rather than a shared/global prototype; there is no `__proto__`/`constructor.prototype` manipulation here, so this is a generic dynamic-property-assignment pattern misclassified as prototype pollution.

This is minified third-party vendor code (jQuery plugin ajax-bootstrap-select v1.4.3) bundled under wwwroot/lib, not application logic. The flag appears to be a generic pattern match on jQuery's `$.extend(true, {}, ...)` deep-merge idiom, which is pervasive in jQuery plugins; the merges shown operate on developer-supplied plugin options and DOM data-* attributes (`data-abs-*`), not on attacker-controlled JSON keys recursively merged into a shared/global object. The one place remote AJAX response

The regex /\s([a-zA-Z]+)/ is a simple linear-time pattern with no nested quantifiers or overlapping alternations, so it cannot exhibit catastrophic backtracking; it's also applied to the fixed string produced by Object.prototype.toString.call(obj), not attacker-controlled input.

This is a single-level assignment (data.options[i] = options[i]) inside vendored third-party bootstrap-select library code; unlike the classic nested target[a][b]=c pattern, assigning to a '__proto__' key here only reassigns the [[Prototype]] of the local data.options object instance, not Object.prototype globally, so it doesn't produce cross-object prototype pollution. There's also no shown untrusted-input path reaching the `_option` argument (it comes from the page's own JS call to the widget)

DATA_API_KEY here is a Bootstrap.js internal constant holding the string '.data-api', a CSS/event-namespace suffix used to identify data-api triggered events, not a credential or secret. This is a mis-classification by the scanner due to the variable name containing 'KEY'.

This is a minified vendored third-party bundle (Popper.js + bootstrap-select), not application code; the regexes present (simple alternations, character classes, fixed-format parsing patterns) are all linear/bounded with no nested quantifiers or catastrophic backtracking potential, and none are applied to attacker-controlled untrusted input in an exploitable way.

The flagged line is inside a minified third-party bootstrap-select library's live-search filter logic; it contains no dynamic property assignment via user-controlled keys into object prototypes (e.g., no `obj[__proto__]` or merge-based assignment from untrusted JSON), so this is a mis-classification/false positive rather than a real prototype pollution sink.

This is vendored third-party library code (bootstrap-select.js) doing a single-level, non-recursive property copy guarded by hasOwnProperty; unlike vulnerable $.extend(true,...) deep-merge patterns, a shallow one-level assignment to data.options[i] cannot reach nested __proto__.constructor.prototype chains needed for global prototype pollution, and the options argument is a developer-supplied plugin config object, not raw untrusted network input.

This is minified third-party bootstrap-select v1.13.0 library code; the flagged line only toggles a CSS 'disabled' class/attribute on a button element and contains no dynamic object property assignment or merge logic that could pollute Object.prototype.

This regex (from a vendored Bootstrap/Popper.js library file) has only sequential, non-nested quantifiers (\d*\.?\d*) followed by .*, which cannot exhibit catastrophic exponential backtracking, and the input (CSS offset value/unit strings) is not attacker-controlled untrusted network input.

This is unmodified third-party Bootstrap v4 bundle code copying config properties that differ from framework defaults into a local plain object; there is no user-controlled key merged into Object.prototype or any application object, so no prototype pollution occurs here.
This is a vendored, unmodified Bootstrap.js library file; DATA_API_KEY is a CSS selector suffix ('.data-api') used to namespace jQuery data-API event bindings, not a credential or secret.

This is minified third-party Bootstrap 4.1.0 vendor library code, not application logic; the flagged line is the UMD wrapper/library bundle itself, and any regex usage within is vetted upstream library code, not attacker-controlled pattern construction.

This is the vendored, unmodified Bootstrap v4.1.0 bundle (third-party library file, not app-authored code); the merge helper uses Object.keys() which does not enumerate a literal __proto__ property, so there's no actual prototype-pollution sink here — the scanner appears to be pattern-matching generic object-merge code in a minified vendor file.

The regex /\s([a-z]+)/i has no nested/overlapping quantifiers and cannot exhibit catastrophic backtracking; additionally its input is the fixed-format string from Object.prototype.toString.call(obj) (e.g. "[object Object]"), not attacker-controlled data, so there is no reachable exploit path.

This is unmodified third-party Bootstrap v4 library code copying own-enumerable config properties from a fixed internal `this.config` object, not user-controlled key input into an object merge/assignment sink; there is no `__proto__`/prototype pollution pattern here.

DATA_API_KEY here is a Bootstrap.js internal constant used to build a CSS/event namespace suffix ('.data-api'), not a secret credential; the value is a UI selector string, not an API key or credential, and this is vendored third-party library code.

This is the vendored, minified Bootstrap 4.1.0 library file, not application code; the flagged regexes (e.g. `new RegExp("38|40|27")`, `new RegExp("(^|\\s)"+ue+"\\S+","g")`) are simple, linear-time patterns with no nested quantifiers or catastrophic backtracking potential, and operate on trusted DOM attribute strings, not attacker-controlled input.

This is unmodified, third-party Bootstrap 4.1.0 library code; the flagged `h()`/extend-like helper uses `Object.defineProperty` and safe key assignment without any `__proto__` traversal, so it does not perform prototype pollution.

This is vendored third-party Font Awesome library code, not application logic; `prefix` is a hardcoded icon-style identifier (e.g. 'fas','far') defined by the library itself, not attacker-controlled input, and the assignment targets `namespace.styles`, an internal FontAwesome object rather than a prototype chain.

This is unmodified vendored Font Awesome Free 5.0.10 minified library code, not application logic. The flagged line is standard namespace initialization (H[v]||(H[v]={})) and an Object.assign polyfill, with no unsanitized merge/extend of user-controlled input into object prototypes — a naive pattern match on minified third-party code, not a real prototype pollution vulnerability.

This is vendored third-party Font Awesome library code, not application logic; `prefix` is an internal, hardcoded style key (e.g. 'fas'/'far'/'fab') from the library's own icon definitions, not attacker-controlled input, and `_extends` performs a shallow merge into a fresh object rather than a recursive merge that could taint `Object.prototype`.

This is unmodified third-party Font Awesome library code (a static vendor asset) that uses `H[l][x]||(H[l][x]={})` bracket-notation initialization on an internal namespace object with a fixed, hardcoded key string ("___FONT_AWESOME___"), not attacker-controlled input; the tool is pattern-matching generic dynamic property assignment, not a real prototype pollution sink.

This is vendored third-party Font Awesome library code merging icon style definitions into namespace.styles[prefix], where prefix is a fixed internal style key (e.g. 'fas'/'far'/'fab'), not attacker-controlled input, and the assignment targets a plain data object rather than Object.prototype or __proto__/constructor chains.

This is unmodified vendor code (Font Awesome's minified JS) where the flagged line only initializes a namespace object under a fixed constant key (`___FONT_AWESOME___`) and includes an Object.assign polyfill that already guards with hasOwnProperty checks — there's no user-controlled key or merge target involved, so it's a pattern-matching false positive on third-party library code, not an exploitable prototype pollution path.

This is vendored third-party Font Awesome library code (wwwroot/lib), and addPack's prefix/icons parameters are library-internal API inputs set by the application's own icon registration calls, not attacker-controlled request data; no untrusted-input reachability is shown, so this looks like a generic obj[key]=value pattern flagged without exploitability.

This is a vendored, minified Font Awesome library file with no user-controlled merge/assignment into object prototypes; the alleged '__proto_pollution__' pattern doesn't correspond to any actual prototype pollution sink in this icon-definition code.

This is vendored third-party Font Awesome library code (wwwroot/lib), and `addPack`'s `icons` argument is normally invoked with static, developer-supplied icon definitions, not attacker-controlled runtime input; even if `icons` had a `__proto__` key, Object.assign-style `_extends({}, ..., icons)` only sets the prototype of the freshly created local object, not the global Object.prototype, so it doesn't constitute the classic CWE-1321 pollution the rule implies.

This is minified, unmodified third-party Font Awesome 5.0.10 vendor code; the scanner's __proto_pollution__ heuristic likely matched generic object-merge/property-assignment patterns (e.g. Object.assign polyfill, O() merge helper) rather than any actual unsanitized merge of attacker-controlled keys into a prototype chain.

This is unmodified vendored third-party library code (jQuery Validation Plugin's additional-methods.js) executing entirely client-side in the browser; even though the regex has real nested-quantifier ambiguity (the label character class includes '.' while the outer group also loops on '.'), any catastrophic backtracking would only hang the browser tab of whoever typed the value into their own form field, not the server or other users, so it's not an exploitable DoS against the application.

This is unmodified, vendored third-party code (jQuery Validation Plugin v1.17.0, minified into one line), not application logic under test; the finding doesn't identify a specific regex, and scanning the many regexes present none exhibit the classic nested-quantifier/ambiguous-alternation pattern needed for catastrophic backtracking. Even if one were exploitable, it executes client-side in the user's own browser, so impact is limited to a single-tab hang rather than a server-side DoS.

This is Diego Perini's widely-used URL validation regex vendored unmodified into jquery-validate; the flagged repetition `(?:[a-z0-9...]-*)*` requires a mandatory single character per outer iteration so dash consumption is unambiguous, avoiding the classic overlapping-quantifier pattern that causes exponential backtracking. It also only runs client-side against a user's own form input, limiting any DoS impact to self.

This is vendored jQuery Validate library code assigning to validator.invalid[element.name], a plain object keyed by a form field name from the DOM, not user-controlled JSON merged via __proto__/constructor.prototype; there's no prototype-pollution gadget here, just a mis-classified property assignment.

This is the unmodified, minified jQuery Validation Plugin v1.17.0 third-party library file, not project-authored code; the flagged line is generic bundler/UMD boilerplate with no attacker-controllable regex construction shown, and the regexes present in the plugin (email/url/date patterns) are fixed, well-known patterns rather than dynamically built from untrusted input.

This is unmodified, minified third-party jQuery Validation Plugin v1.17.0 vendor code; the flagged pattern is a generic minifier artifact/heuristic mismatch, not an actual use of `__proto__` merge on untrusted keys, and it's a static vendored library file, not application logic reachable with attacker-controlled property names.

The regex is a simple single-character-class alternation with no nested quantifiers or overlapping alternations, so it matches in linear time with no catastrophic backtracking potential; it's also jQuery's official escaping helper for selector strings, not a novel untrusted-pattern construction.

This is unmodified third-party jQuery Validation Unobtrusive library code building a plain per-form validation-options dictionary keyed by element.name (a DOM attribute set by the page author); it never touches Object.prototype or a globally shared/prototype-chain object, so the 'prototype pollution' classification is a mischaracterization of ordinary object property assignment in vendored library code.

This is vendored, minified third-party library code (jQuery Validation Unobtrusive v3.2.9) with no complex/nested-quantifier regex patterns (only simple trim/split/escape/dynamic regex from developer-defined validation attributes, not attacker-controlled request data at exploitation time); there's no evidence of catastrophic backtracking or remote reachability from untrusted input.

This is unmodified third-party Microsoft jQuery Validation Unobtrusive v3.2.9 library code; the shown code contains no object-merge/assignment into attacker-controlled keys or `__proto__`/`constructor.prototype` sink, so the generic tool message does not correspond to any visible exploitable pollution path.

This line assigns the well-known Symbol.iterator from the native Array prototype to jQuery.fn as a one-time, hardcoded library initialization step; it involves no user-controlled key or merge/copy of untrusted object properties, so it cannot pollute Object.prototype. The rule appears to be a naive pattern match on bracket-notation property assignment rather than a genuine prototype-pollution sink.

This is unmodified vendored jQuery library code; the regex /^(none|table(?!-c[ea]).+)/ has no nested/overlapping quantifiers that could cause catastrophic backtracking (linear-time matching), and it's applied internally to CSS display-property strings, not attacker-controlled arbitrary-length input.

This is unmodified jQuery library source code assigning methods onto jQuery.fn using a fixed, hardcoded list of event names split from a string literal (not user-controlled input), so there is no attacker-controlled key reaching an object property assignment; it's a mis-classification by the scanner on vendored third-party code.

This is the entire minified jQuery 3.3.1 library vendored under wwwroot/lib, not application code; the finding is a generic pattern match on regex usage within third-party code with no specific vulnerable regex or exploitable input path identified.

This is minified jQuery 3.3.1 vendor library code, not application code; the pattern matcher flagged generic property-assignment code (w.extend, etc.) as prototype pollution without evidence of unsanitized untrusted input reaching a dangerous merge, and jQuery's extend is well-known, widely audited third-party code not authored by the project.

This is jQuery's built-in rdisplayswap regex applied only to internal CSS display-style strings (not attacker-controlled network input); the pattern `^(none|table(?!-c[ea]).+)` has a single trailing `.+` with no nested/overlapping quantifiers, so it runs in linear time and cannot exhibit catastrophic backtracking.

This is unmodified jQuery slim library code that assigns shorthand methods (click, blur, focus, etc.) onto jQuery.fn using a fixed, hardcoded list of event names split from a string literal — there is no attacker-controlled key or merge/copy of untrusted object properties involved, so it cannot cause prototype pollution.

This is the unmodified, minified jQuery 3.3.1 library file (a third-party vendor dependency), not application code; any regex complexity here is inherent to a widely-used, well-audited library and not an application-specific defect reachable via untrusted input in this codebase.

This is jQuery 3.3.1 (vulnerable version, per the banner and version string 'x=3.3.1'), and the shown extend()/deep-merge implementation matches the known CVE-2019-11358 pattern: it recursively copies own-enumerable keys from a source object into the target without excluding '__proto__' or 'constructor', so `$.extend(true, {}, JSON.parse(untrustedInput))` can pollute Object.prototype. Confidence is moderate rather than high because exploitability ultimately depends on whether application code ca

The extend() function copies all enumerable keys from fns into obj via obj[s]=fns[s] with no denylist for __proto__/constructor/prototype, matching the classic unsafe-merge gadget (this matches tinysort's known prototype-pollution advisory); exploitability depends on whether callers ever pass attacker-influenced options objects to tinysort, which isn't visible here.

This is the minified, vendored TinySort library; the flagged pattern is its internal option-merge helper `r(e,t,r){for(var n in t)(r||e[n]===o)&&(e[n]=t[n])}` which copies developer-supplied sort options (not attacker-controlled JSON) into an internal defaults object — there's no reachable path from untrusted input to a `__proto__`/`constructor.prototype` key, so the generic prototype-pollution heuristic is mis-firing on a benign extend/merge pattern in third-party library code.

The POST Edit action performs a state-changing save (_movieManager.Save(vm)) but the [ValidateAntiForgeryToken] attribute is explicitly commented out (with a comment acknowledging it should be added), leaving the endpoint unprotected against CSRF.

The method passes an unvalidated user-supplied query string directly to _movieManager.GetPersonSelectData(query), but the implementation of that manager method (and whether it builds a SQL/LDAP/other injectable expression from the input) is not shown, so it's impossible to confirm an actual injection sink from this code alone.

DtdProcessing is explicitly set to Parse and XmlResolver is set to a live XmlUrlResolver, enabling DTD/external entity resolution on attacker-controlled XML input passed into the reader, which is the canonical XXE misconfiguration (reinforced by the commented-out exploit payloads referencing local file and SSRF entities).

The flagged line 56 is blank whitespace with no SQL query, string concatenation, or data access of any kind; the nearby code only calls accessor methods (_movieAccessor.Get, _userMovieAccessor.GetByMovieAndUser) whose implementation isn't shown, so there's no evidence of SQL injection at this location.

escapedArgs is interpolated unquoted into ProcessStartInfo.Arguments passed to certutil; even though UseShellExecute=false avoids shell metacharacter injection, an attacker-controlled value containing spaces/dashes can inject additional certutil flags (e.g. -urlcache) enabling command/argument injection via a known LOLBin, and no argument-safe escaping for process invocation is visible in the shown code.

The `cmd` parameter is used directly (after only quote-escaping, not path-traversal sanitization) to build a file path passed to File.WriteAllText, allowing an attacker-controlled value like '../../etc/passwd' or similar to write files outside the intended directory.

request.UserId and request.Search are concatenated directly into a raw SQL INSERT statement executed via ExecuteSqlRaw without parameterization, allowing SQL injection from user-controlled search/request input.
This is a simple boolean property getter comparing PageIndex to a constant; there is no SQL query construction or database interaction anywhere in the shown code, so CWE-89 does not apply.
